title: Black Power, Law & Policy in DC
summary: Trace Black legal strategy, civic power, and home-rule leadership across Howard Law, major public institutions, and the core of District government.

stops.title: Howard University School of Law
stops.description: Start with the Black legal tradition that trained generations of civil-rights lawyers, policy thinkers, and movement strategists.

stops.title: Thurgood Marshall Center
stops.description: Bridge legal power and civic memory at the old Dunbar High School building, now home to Black institutional convening and policy work.

stops.title: Martin Luther King Jr. Memorial Library
stops.description: Center the route in public knowledge, civic literacy, and the archival life of organizing inside one of DC's most visible public institutions.

stops.title: Marion S. Barry, Jr. Building
stops.description: Read Black local political power through the legacy of Marion Barry and the city's long fight to govern itself on its own terms.

stops.title: John A. Wilson Building
stops.description: Close at the seat of District government, where home rule, hearings, executive power, and city policy all meet.

title: U Street Black Arts & Nightlife
summary: Follow Black Broadway through freedom memory, jazz rooms, neighborhood institutions, and the nightlife corridor that shaped modern Black DC culture.

stops.title: African American Civil War Memorial
stops.description: Open the route with a freedom anchor that places U Street inside a longer story of Black service, sacrifice, and public memory.

stops.title: True Reformer Building
stops.description: Read U Street as a corridor of Black institution-building, business ambition, and civic infrastructure before the music fully takes over.

stops.title: Ben's Chili Bowl
stops.description: Hold neighborhood continuity, movement memory, and everyday social life at one of the corridor's most enduring gathering places.

stops.title: Lincoln Theatre
stops.description: Anchor the route in Black Broadway stage history, performance glamour, and the public face of U Street's cultural power.

stops.title: Bohemian Caverns
stops.description: Move into the more intimate jazz-club side of the corridor, where performance history lived below street level and late into the night.

stops.title: Howard Theatre
stops.description: Close with one of DC's defining Black performance landmarks, where neighborhood identity and national entertainment history meet.

title: Howard, Shaw & Black Intellectual Capital
summary: Trace Black intellectual life through Howard University, archives, sacred space, medicine, and elite public education in the neighborhoods around Shaw.

stops.title: Howard University Main Gate
stops.description: Begin at the threshold of the institution that made Black scholarship, leadership, and professional formation central to the city.

stops.title: Founders Library
stops.description: Use Howard's symbolic core to frame the route around archives, intellectual ambition, and the built language of Black institutional confidence.

stops.title: Moorland-Spingarn Research Center
stops.description: Stop at one of the country's great Black archival centers, where scholarship, memory, and collection-building become part of the route itself.

stops.title: Andrew Rankin Memorial Chapel
stops.description: Add moral leadership, ceremony, and public voice to the route through one of Howard's most recognizable civic-spiritual spaces.

stops.title: Cramton Auditorium
stops.description: Bring in lectures, assemblies, campus culture, and the performance of public ideas inside Howard's collective gathering spaces.

stops.title: Howard University Hospital
stops.description: Read Black medical training, care infrastructure, and the Freedmen's Hospital lineage through a living institutional site.

stops.title: Dunbar High School
stops.description: Close with Black public-school excellence, showing that intellectual capital in DC extended beyond campus and into the citywide education tradition.

title: Freedom on the Mall
summary: Move from Black women's organizing into national memory, protest space, and civil-rights commemoration across the Mall's most resonant public landmarks.

stops.title: Mary McLeod Bethune Council House
stops.description: Open with Black women's leadership, organizing strategy, and the institutional groundwork that helped shape freedom politics in Washington.

stops.title: National Museum of African American History and Culture
stops.description: Step into the national archive of Black memory, struggle, culture, and achievement at the Mall's clearest institutional anchor for this story.

stops.title: Washington Monument Grounds
stops.description: Read the Mall as protest space, where marches, assemblies, and mass public presence turned national symbolism into democratic pressure.

stops.title: Lincoln Memorial
stops.description: Hold both Marian Anderson's 1939 performance and King's 1963 speech at a site where Black claims on the nation became impossible to ignore.

stops.title: Martin Luther King, Jr. Memorial
stops.description: Close with a memorial that turns movement into national civic memory while keeping the unfinished work of freedom in view.

title: Go-Go, Neighborhood Memory & Everyday Black DC
summary: Follow Black DC's living pulse through go-go memory, neighborhood creative spaces, Ward 8 reinvestment, Chuck Brown's public legacy, and the city's present-day cultural stage.

stops.title: Go-Go Museum & Cafe
stops.description: Open with a space devoted to the city's homegrown sound, where go-go is framed as local memory, public rhythm, and cultural inheritance.

stops.title: Anacostia Arts Center
stops.description: Read neighborhood creativity through storefront art, small business energy, and the everyday cultural infrastructure that keeps Black DC visible.

stops.title: MLK Gateway
stops.description: Use this Ward 8 reinvestment corridor to think about how Black neighborhoods carry memory forward while still negotiating change, development, and ownership.

stops.title: Chuck Brown Memorial Park
stops.description: Hold go-go's most public hometown tribute at the park named for the city's defining bandleader and cultural ambassador.

stops.title: CareFirst Arena
stops.description: Close at the arena that keeps go-go in contemporary city branding, showing how the sound still travels through present-day DC life.
